items.question_text: When not being radar monitored, GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV routes must be cleared via what type of route?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c1: When not being radar monitored, GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on random RNAV routes must be cleared via or reported to be established on a point-to-point route.

choices.text: Via direct impromptu routing from departure to destination.

items.question_text: For nonradar separation on random RNAV routes, what is the maximum distance between points and how much airspace must be protected either side of the route centerline?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c1(a)(b): Maximum distance between points must not exceed 500 miles. Protect 4 miles either side of the route centerline.

items.question_text: For random RNAV routes under nonradar conditions, assigned altitudes must be at or above what minimum?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c1(c): Assigned altitudes must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment being flown, including the protected airspace of that route segment.

choices.text: The highest MIA along the projected route segment including protected airspace.
The MOCA for the entire route of flight.
FL 180 minimum for all random RNAV routes.

items.question_text: What is the difference between a random impromptu route and a point-to-point route per 4-4-1?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c2/c3: A random impromptu routing is a direct course from present position to a fix, initiated by ATC or pilot during flight. A point-to-point route segment begins and ends with a published NAVAID, waypoint, fix, or airport.

choices.text: Impromptu routes are pre-filed; point-to-point routes are assigned in the air.
Impromptu routes are direct from present position; point-to-point routes begin and end with published fixes.
Point-to-point routes require radar; impromptu routes do not.
There is no difference; they are synonyms.

items.question_text: When clearing an aircraft via a substitute ATS route, what word must immediately precede the alternative route?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1a phraseology: 'SUBSTITUTE (ATS route) FROM (fix) to (fix).' The word 'substitute' precedes the route.

items.question_text: When defining fixes/waypoints by latitude/longitude coordinates, how must the coordinates be stated?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1j3: State latitude and longitude in degrees and minutes including the direction from the axis such as North or West. Phraseology: 'DIRECT (number degrees) DEGREES, (number minutes) MINUTES (north or south), (number degrees) DEGREES, (number minutes) MINUTES (east or west).'

choices.text: In degrees and minutes including the direction (N/S, E/W).
In degrees only, rounded to the nearest degree.

items.question_text: A fix defined as an offset from a published ATS route uses what phraseology?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1j4: 'OFFSET (distance) RIGHT/LEFT OF (route).' Example: 'Offset eight miles right of Victor six.'

items.question_text: When clearing RNAV aircraft between designated ATS routes via random RNAV routes, what must be provided and what is the exception?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-2g: Clear RNAV aircraft between ATS routes via random RNAV routes to a fix on the new route. Provide radar monitoring to aircraft transitioning via random RNAV routes. Exception: point-to-point routes via an impromptu route.

choices.text: Radar monitoring is always required with no exceptions.
Provide radar monitoring, except for point-to-point routes via an impromptu route.
Radar monitoring is optional; pilot navigation is primary.
No radar monitoring needed if the aircraft is GNSS-equipped.

items.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a listed method for effecting route structure transitions per 4-4-2?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-2, methods are: (a) vector to/from radials, (b) SID/STAR, (c) climb/descend via radials, (d) direct to/between NAVAIDs, (e) climb/descend via ATS route, (f) climb/descend on specified radials, (g) RNAV between routes via random RNAV routes. Holding patterns are not listed.

choices.text: Vector aircraft to or from radials, courses, or azimuths of the assigned ATS route.
Place the aircraft in a holding pattern at the transition fix.
Clear RNAV aircraft between routes via random RNAV routes.

items.question_text: What three components must a degree-distance fix contain in a military flight plan?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a1: A degree-distance fix is composed of (a) a location identifier, (b) azimuth in degrees magnetic, and (c) distance in miles from the NAVAID used. Example: 'MKE 030025.'

choices.text: Location identifier, azimuth in degrees true, and distance in nautical miles.
Location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, and distance in miles.
Airport identifier, radial in degrees, and DME distance.
Fix name, bearing from NAVAID, and flight level.

items.question_text: For military degree-distance route definitions below FL 180, what is the maximum distance between fixes?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a3: Below FL 180 the max distance is 80 miles. FL 180 and above is 260 miles. Celestial navigation routes at all altitudes is 260 miles.

items.question_text: Degree-distance fixes used to define a route are considered what type of reporting point?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a4: Degree-distance fixes used to define a route must be considered compulsory reporting points except that an aircraft may be authorized by ATC to omit reports when traffic conditions permit.

choices.text: Compulsory reporting points, but may be omitted with ATC authorization when traffic permits.

items.question_text: Military aircraft using degree-distance route definitions must file flight plans at least how long before ETD?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a5(b): Flight plans must be filed at least 2 hours before the estimated time of departure.

items.question_text: Within how many miles of the departure and arrival airports must military aircraft using degree-distance routes conform to normal traffic flow?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a5(a): Unless prior coordination has been effected, flight plan the departure and arrival phases to conform with routine traffic flow when operating within 75 miles of the departure and arrival airport.

items.question_text: For degree-distance routes at FL 180 and above, what is the maximum distance between fixes?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3a3(b): At FL 180 and above, the maximum distance is 260 miles.

items.question_text: Which of the following special military operations is NOT authorized to define routes by degree-distance fixes per 4-4-3b?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-3b: Authorized operations include airborne radar navigation/RBS/missile programming, celestial navigation, target aircraft with interceptors, missions above FL 450, USN fighters in positive control, USN/USMC TACAN in Honolulu FIR, MTR aircraft, and AMC SKE routes. Routine ANG training flights are not listed.

choices.text: Airborne radar navigation and radar bomb scoring conducted by USAF/USN/RAF.
Routine Air National Guard training flights.
USAF Air Mobility Command aircraft on approved SKE routes.

items.question_text: When a NAVAID makes part of an airway unusable, which aircraft must be cleared via alternative routes?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-4: Clear aircraft that are not RNAV capable via alternative routes. RNAV aircraft are not subject to this paragraph's alternative route requirement.

choices.text: Aircraft that are not RNAV capable.

items.question_text: When issuing an alternative route clearance using a charted route, what word must immediately precede the route?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-4a: Use the word 'substitute' immediately preceding the alternative route in issuing the clearance.

items.question_text: What should the controller do for RNAV aircraft regarding a NAVAID outage?
items.explanation: Per the 4-4-4 note: Inform area navigation aircraft that will proceed to the NAVAID location of the NAVAID outage.

choices.text: Issue them an alternative route clearance.
Inform them that they will proceed to the NAVAID location of the outage.
Clear them direct to an alternate NAVAID.
No action is needed for RNAV aircraft.

items.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a listed alternative route method when an airway is unusable due to NAVAID status?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-4, alternative route methods are: (a) route on charts using 'substitute', (b) NAVAID radials/courses/azimuths, (c) direct to/between NAVAIDs, (d) vectors. SIDs/STARs are not listed as alternative route methods.

choices.text: A route defined by specifying NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.
A route defined as direct to or between NAVAIDs.
Assign a SID or STAR as the alternative route.

items.question_text: When should routes through Class G airspace be included in a clearance?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-5: Include routes through Class G airspace only when requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Always, for IFR aircraft transiting the area.
Only when requested by the pilot.
When the route is filed in the flight plan regardless of pilot request.

items.question_text: Separation criteria apply in Class G airspace to which aircraft?
items.explanation: Per the note in 4-4-5: Separation criteria are not applicable in Class G airspace. Traffic advisories and safety alerts are applicable within Class G airspace to aircraft in direct communication with ATC.

choices.text: Separation criteria are not applicable in Class G airspace.
Only aircraft on IFR flight plans.

items.question_text: A flight plan filed for a random RNAV route through Class G airspace is considered what?
items.explanation: Per the note in 4-4-5: Flight plans filed for random RNAV routes through Class G airspace are considered a request by the pilot.

choices.text: An invalid route that must be amended.
A request by the pilot to include Class G airspace in the clearance.
An automatic IFR clearance through the airspace.
A requirement for special VFR operations.

items.question_text: When must a controller NOT issue a routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route (per 4-4-6a)?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-6a: Unless operational necessity dictates, do not issue a routing clearance that will take an aircraft off its flight plan route if: (1) the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative, or (2) the route is within a protected segment.

choices.text: When the aircraft is below 10,000 feet.
When the aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative or within a protected segment.
When the aircraft is in Class B airspace.
Whenever the pilot has not specifically requested direct routing.

items.question_text: If a routing within a protected segment must be amended, with whom must coordination be accomplished for an ARTCC TMU amending a route outside their facility?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-6a2(c): For ARTCC TMU amendments outside their facility, coordinate with ATCSCC. ATCS coordinates with TMU. Terminal TMU coordinates with overlying ARTCC TMU.

items.question_text: EN ROUTE: When may a controller issue a revised routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in the facility's airspace?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-6b: Do not issue revised routing clearances past the last fix in your airspace unless requested by the pilot or operational necessity dictates.

choices.text: Any time the controller deems it efficient.
Only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.
Only when the receiving facility has accepted the handoff.

items.question_text: What does the note in 4-4-6 clarify about LOA/SOP routing clearances?
items.explanation: Per the note in 4-4-6: Nothing in this paragraph precludes a controller from issuing a routing clearance that conforms to an LOA or SOP within their facility or between facilities, is required to maintain separation, or comply with traffic flow management initiatives.

choices.text: LOA/SOP routing clearances are always prohibited by this paragraph.
LOA/SOP routing clearances, separation-required clearances, and TM flow compliance clearances are not precluded.
Only separation-related routing changes are permitted.
LOAs override all other routing restrictions.

items.instruction: Distinguish between random impromptu and point-to-point route types.
items.question_text: An RNAV aircraft is airborne and ATC clears it from its present position direct to the Modesto VORTAC. What type of random route is this?
items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c2: A random impromptu routing is a direct course from present position to a fix, initiated during flight. Point-to-point routes begin and end with published fixes on both ends.
